category_title: First-grade struggling readers who experience cognitive overload and blank out when initiating speech-to-text dictation
student_description: David is a first-grade struggling reader who experiences severe cognitive overload when transitioning from brainstorming to using speech recognition (SR) technology. The mechanical act of launching the app causes him to 'go blank' and forget his ideas, and he quickly abandons tasks or deletes entire drafts when faced with transcription errors or cumbersome editing tools. He uniquely needs immediate verbal scaffolding to hold his ideas in working memory, swift teacher intervention to bypass technical hurdles, and a shift away from using SR for full-sentence drafting toward generating word banks and messy drafts.

criteria.rationale: Using SR for full sentences causes cognitive overload for David, whereas using it for single words captures his oral vocabulary without the pressure of sentence mechanics.
criteria.check: The long-term plan includes using speech recognition to generate composition-specific word banks during brainstorming rather than exclusively for drafting full sentences.

criteria.rationale: David views his spoken story as 'already done' and gets frustrated by errors; he needs to see drafting as a distinct, messy stage.
criteria.check: The long-term plan incorporates modeling the drafting process on alternative mediums (like chart paper) using invented spelling and pattern notations to demonstrate that drafts are not final products.

criteria.rationale: Building peer discussion around SR inaccuracies transforms a source of individual frustration into a normalized, collective problem-solving activity.
criteria.check: The long-term plan includes teaching David how to have purposeful peer conversations to examine and troubleshoot SR inaccuracies.
